primary.hook:
The riskiest AI thing in your company next year won’t be the model.
It’ll be the agents you quietly wired into production systems.
core_idea: Most teams are still thinking about AI security as "prompt + data" and ignoring the fact that agents are effectively unreviewed junior engineers with API keys.
In real projects, the worst risks come from how agents are orchestrated: tool permissions, autonomy, and lack of observability — not from the base model itself.
There are four recurring security mistakes in agent projects: insecure tools, over-trusted autonomy, no audit trail, and shadow agents built outside governance.
Treating agents as "smart endpoints" instead of "unreliable interns" leads to non-obvious failure modes: lateral movement via tools, business logic abuse, and untraceable incidents.
You can reduce 80% of the risk with a few simple patterns: least-privilege tools, constrained interfaces, forced logging, and an explicit inventory of agents and their blast radius.
primary.draft_post:
CAROUSEL TITLE
Are AI Agents Your Next Security Nightmare?
The 4 security mistakes I keep seeing in real projects

Intro (post copy above the carousel)
Everyone is busy doing "AI risk reviews" of models and data.
Meanwhile, agents are getting:
– direct database access
– production API keys
– permissions in Slack / Jira / GitHub

And almost nobody is threat-modeling the orchestration layer.

Here are the 4 security mistakes I keep seeing in real agent projects — and the patterns that actually work.

Swipe through ↓

---
Slide 1 — The uncomfortable truth
Title: Your most dangerous "employee" is a JSON config file

Body:
When you add tools to an agent, you’re not just improving UX.
You’re giving a stochastic system the ability to:
– change data
– trigger workflows
– touch real customers

Most teams lock down the model and the data.
Almost nobody locks down the actions.

This is where the real security story starts.

---
Slide 2 — Mistake #1
Title: Treating tools as toys, not capabilities
Subtitle: "It’s just a POC"… wired into production

What goes wrong:
– One shared service account for all tools
– Agents can call any tool from any context
– No environment separation (dev/stage/prod)

Real pattern I keep seeing:
– A "run_sql" tool with full prod access
– No row-level security
– No query whitelisting or approval step

What to do instead:
– Design tools as capabilities with a blast radius
– One agent = one role = scoped permissions
– Separate credentials per environment
– Default to read-only tools; add write only when a human approves

Non-obvious pattern:
The biggest risk window is the "experimental" phase — that’s when people bypass all the normal security processes.

---
Slide 3 — Mistake #2
Title: Over-trusting agent autonomy
Subtitle: You gave it root access and prayed the prompt was enough

What goes wrong:
– Assuming "the model won’t do that"
– Free-form natural language → powerful tools with no guardrails
– No business-logic checks on tool arguments

Example pattern:
– Agent can:
  – create tickets
  – send emails
  – adjust discounts
– The *only* control is natural language instructions in the system prompt.

This isn’t a guardrail. It’s a wish.

What to do instead:
– Wrap tools with explicit schemas and hard constraints
– Validate all tool inputs against business rules
– Require human-in-the-loop for high-impact actions (money, access, data deletion)
– Treat the agent as an unreliable intern whose work must be checked

Non-obvious pattern:
Most "prompt injection" risks become much worse when the agent has tools that modify state.

---
Slide 4 — Mistake #3
Title: No audit trail for what the agent actually did
Subtitle: You can’t secure what you can’t reconstruct

What goes wrong:
– Only log final user-visible output
– No record of intermediate prompts, tool calls, or responses
– No linkage between a user, a request, and the chain of actions

Real impact:
– You can’t debug bad behavior
– You can’t do forensics after an incident
– You can’t improve the agent systematically

What to do instead:
Log at the orchestration level:
– Full prompt + tool call history (with redaction where needed)
– Who/what initiated each request
– Which credentials / environment were used
– Outcome of each tool call (including failures)

Non-obvious pattern:
Most orgs have better logging for a cron job that runs once a day than for an agent that can touch thousands of records an hour.

---
Slide 5 — Mistake #4
Title: Shadow agents: the new shadow IT
Subtitle: "It’s just a small automation" (with production access)

What goes wrong:
– Teams spin up agents in:
  – notebooks
  – Zapier / n8n
  – internal scripts
– They reuse prod API keys they already have
– Security and platform teams never hear about it

Pattern I keep seeing:
– First: one-off "helper" agent that writes Slack updates
– Then: it gets tool access to ticketing / CRM / billing
– Six months later: nobody knows what it can actually do

What to do instead:
– Maintain an inventory of agents:
  – where they run
  – which tools they have
  – who owns them
– Require a minimal threat model for any agent with write access
– Make "agent reviews" part of your existing change/governance process

Non-obvious pattern:
Most agent-related incidents in the next 12–18 months will come from these shadow systems, not the flagship AI product.

---
Slide 6 — Putting it together
Title: A simple mental model: agents as untrusted code + powerful tools

If you only remember one thing, make it this:

An agent is not a UI feature.
It’s untrusted code that:
– writes its own plans
– chooses its own tools
– executes real actions

Minimum viable security for agents:
1) Least-privilege tools with explicit blast radius
2) Hard constraints + validation on tool inputs
3) Orchestration-level logging and traceability
4) An actual inventory of agents and who owns them

If you’re already building with agents:
– Which of these 4 mistakes are you making today?
– What would break first if the agent stopped following the prompt?

That’s where your security work should start.
primary.visual_suggestion:
Clean, slightly stark design with a "systems" feel. Use a consistent color for "danger" elements (e.g., red accents) and diagrams instead of stock photos.

Slide-specific visuals:
- Slide 1: Simple diagram: User → Agent → Tools → Systems, with the tools/systems side highlighted in red as the real risk area.
- Slide 2: Table with two columns: "How teams model tools" (toy icons, question marks) vs. "What tools actually are" (keys, database, workflow icons).
- Slide 3: Sequence diagram showing an agent taking free-form input and calling a powerful tool; highlight the missing validation layer.
- Slide 4: Timeline or flow diagram where only the final output is logged vs. full chain-of-thought + tool calls; greyed-out gaps to show lack of observability.
- Slide 5: Org chart style graphic with multiple small "shadow" agent icons around the edge, outside the official platform boundary box.
- Slide 6: Box labeled "Agent" connected to tools/APIs with warning triangles; on the side, a concise checklist of the 4 mitigations as the visual focal point.
primary.why_this_works:
- Positions you as someone who actually builds and ships agent systems, not just someone repeating model-level security talking points.
- Focuses on what people get wrong in real projects: tool scoping, autonomy, observability, and governance — all under-discussed compared to "prompt injection" headlines.
- Uses concrete, recognizable patterns (shared service accounts, POCs wired to prod, missing logs, shadow IT) that practitioners will have seen, which builds credibility.
- The non-obvious insights (experimentation phase is riskiest, shadow agents as main incident source, agents as untrusted code rather than "features") differentiate this from generic AI security content.
- Carousel format lets you isolate each mistake and recommended pattern, making it easy for eng leads / data teams to share internally as a lightweight checklist.

source_refs.title: Are AI Agents Your Next Security Nightmare?

passed_checks: No generic motivation or self-help content
Every slide contains at least one mistake, tradeoff, or non-obvious pattern
Explicitly focuses on what people get wrong, not just definitions
Tone is concrete, practitioner-oriented, and non-hype
Ties security concerns to orchestration, tools, and governance rather than only models/data
Includes practical countermeasures without turning into a checklist post
critic_notes: Does not go deep into specific technical mitigations (e.g., policy engines, sandboxing patterns), which could be a follow-up post
Assumes some familiarity with agents/tools; absolute beginners may need a simpler primer first
Examples are generic rather than tied to a single detailed case study, which could further increase perceived depth in a future post
Hook is specific, contrarian, and clearly framed around a concrete emerging risk (agents with tools) rather than generic ‘AI is risky’ talk.
Content focuses on orchestration-layer security (tools, autonomy, observability, governance), which is still underrepresented vs. model/prompt-only discussions — this is not beginner-level framing.

Each slide surfaces at least one non-trivial mistake or pattern that reflects real implementations: shared service accounts, prod wired into POCs, missing business-logic validation, lack of orchestration-level logging, and shadow agents.

The ‘uncomfortable truth’ framing (JSON config as the most dangerous employee; agents as untrusted code, not UI) is opinionated and grounded in systems thinking, not shallow explanation of what agents are.

The non-obvious insights called out explicitly (experimentation phase as highest-risk, cron-job logging vs. agent logging, shadow agents as primary incident source) are the kind of thing you only see from real project exposure.

Mitigations are not generic (“be secure”) — they’re practical and implementation-shaped (per-env credentials, read-only defaults, schema constraints, business-rule validation, inventory of agents & blast radius).
Tone is sharp and practitioner-oriented, avoids hype and platitudes, and does not drift into motivational content or entry-level AI safety tropes.
The structure fits a high-signal carousel: each slide isolates one failure mode + practice, and the close ties it together with a clear mental model (agents as untrusted code + powerful tools).

backups.title: Your RAG System Isn’t "Intelligent" — It’s a Fancy String Search with Expensive Latency
backups.angle: Non-obvious failure modes in production RAG systems
backups.hook:
Most "RAG systems" I review aren’t retrieval-augmented generation.
They’re just costly keyword search glued to an LLM.
backups.why_now: Everyone is rolling out RAG as the default pattern, but most implementations quietly fail: irrelevant chunks, no evaluation, and retrieval stacks that don’t match the questions users actually ask. This lets you dissect those patterns from a data/ML mindset and show how to reason about retrieval quality, not just stick a vector DB in the diagram.
backups.visual_suggestion: Carousel breaking down a typical RAG diagram into what people think it does vs. what actually happens. Use side-by-side panels: "Architecture deck" vs. "Runtime reality" with issues like bad chunking, naive top-k, and no feedback loops highlighted in red.

backups.title: When "Just Add an LLM" Quietly Destroys Your Data Platform
backups.angle: Tradeoffs and hidden costs of bolting LLMs onto existing analytics stacks
backups.hook:
LLMs don’t magically fix broken data platforms.
They just hide the problems long enough to make them more expensive.
backups.why_now: A lot of teams are dropping LLMs on top of messy warehouses and BI layers. It demos well, but creates brittle query generation, weird caching problems, and an unmaintainable permission model. This post would map the recurring anti-patterns you’ve seen bridging analytics and LLM systems, and show how to design around them instead of papering over them.
backups.visual_suggestion: Before/after architecture diagrams: "Classic analytics stack" vs. "LLM-bolted stack" with tangled arrows. Highlight where governance, lineage, and performance degrade once the LLM is dropped in as an unplanned middle layer.

generated_content.selected_topic_reason: Security around AI agents is under-discussed compared to model and data risk, but it’s where real damage happens once agents get tool access. This topic lets you speak as a builder who’s seen actual implementations: the subtle but recurring mistakes around tool permissions, agent autonomy, missing observability, and shadow agents. It’s also a natural fit for a carousel focused on "what people get wrong" and non-obvious patterns, positioning you as someone who thinks about systems and failure modes rather than just model capabilities.

signals.title: Tell HN: Fiverr left customer files public and searchable
signals.excerpt:
Fiverr (gig work/task platform, competitor to Upwork) uses a service called Cloudinary to process PDF/images in messaging, including work products from the worker to client.

Besides the PDF processing value add, Cloudinary effectively acts like S3 here, serving assets directly to the web client. Like S3, it has support for signed/expiring URLs. However, Fiverr opted to use public URLs, not signed ones, for sensitive client-worker communication.

Moreover, it seems like they may be serving public HTML somewhere that links to these files. As a result, hundreds are in Google search results, many containing PII.

Example query: site:fiverr-res.cloudinary.com form 1040

In fact, Fiverr actively buys Google Ads for keywords like "form 1234 filing" despite knowing that it does not adequately secure the resulting work product, causing the preparer to violate the GLBA/FTC Safeguards Rule.

Responsible Disclosure Note -- 40 days have passed since this was notified to the designated vulnerability email (security@fiverr.com). The security team did not reply. Therefore, this is being made public as it doesn't seem eligible for CVE/CERT processing as it is not really a code vulnerability, and I don't know anyone else who would care about it.

Comments URL: https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=47769796

Points: 430
